content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

May 15, 1995

David L. Malcolm
Port Commissioner
Port of San Diego
P.O. Box 488
San Diego, CA  92112

Re:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-95-137

Dear Mr. Malcolm:

     This is in response to your letter requesting advice
regarding your responsibilities as a Port of San Diego Port
Commissioner under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

     Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place. In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

     May you participate, as a Port Commissioner for the Port of
San Diego, in the consideration of a proposal to build a
performing arts center on land owned by the Port of San Diego?

CONCLUSION

     According to your facts you will not have a conflict of
interest in the decision to permit construction of a performing
arts center on land owned by the Port of San Diego.

_____________________

¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, sections
18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-95-137
Page 2


FACTS

You are a member of the Board of Port Commissioners for the
Port of San Diego (the port commission). The port commission will
be considering a proposal to build a performing arts center on
land owned by the Port of San Diego (held in trust for the State
of California). The proponent of the performing arts center has
hired Steve Delaney as a consultant (one of several) to determine
whether the land in question is suitable for the project.

In March 1995, you and thirteen other persons invested in a
California Limited Liability Corporation (LLC) that is a
franchisee of a Rally's restaurant in San Diego. You stated that
all the partners bought into the corporation at the time it was
created. You purchased an 18.5 percent interest. A living trust
owned by Mr. Delaney purchased a two and one-half percent
interest. Neither you nor Mr. Delaney are employees of the LLC,
and neither of you received any money in the formation of the LLC.
The LLC is managed by the corporate Executive Officer Rene Pratts.

You have asked whether your interest in the LLC creates a
conflict of interest for you with respect to the performing arts
center decision.

ANALYSIS

Economic Interests

The Act was adopted by the voters in California in 1974. The
purpose for the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act was
to ensure that public officials, whether elected or appointed,
would perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias
caused by their own financial interests or the financial interests
of persons who have supported them. (Section 81000(b).)

In furtherance of this goal, Section 87100 of the Act
prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest. Section 87103 specifies that a public
official has a financial interest in a decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally, on the official or a member of his or her immediate
family or on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

* * *

Our File No. A-95-137
Page 3


          (c)  Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

* * *

     For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official, by an agent on behalf of a
public official, or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official's agents,
spouse, and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.

     A.  The Limited Liability Corporation

     Clearly, you have an interest in the LLC by virtue of your
investment interest. Thus, pursuant to Sections 87100 and 87103,
you are prohibited from participating in any governmental decision
that will have a foreseeable and material financial effect on the
LLC.

     B.  Mr. Delaney as an Economic Interest

     Additionally, you have asked about Mr. Delaney who is a
partner in the LLC. You asked whether you have an "economic
interest" in Mr. Delaney as a result of the shared ownership
interest in the LLC.

     Generally, the fact that an official owns an interest in a
business does not mean that the official has an interest in all
the other owners of the business entity. However, in In re Nord
(1983) 8 FPPC Ops. 6, the Commission found that under some
circumstances a partnership interest could result in an investment
interest in controlling general partners of the partnership.

     For example, the Commission stated that in a limited
partnership, the framework of the partnership "involves an
investment by the limited partners in the entrepreneurial skills
of the general partner, who is charged with the sole discretion
and authority to manage the investment by conducting the
partnership business." (Nord, supra, p. 9.) "In doing so, the
limited partner is really investing in the general partner"
personally. (Nord, supra, p. 12.) The Commission concluded in
light of the Act's clear intention to prohibit conflicts of

Our File No. A-95-137
Page 4


interest arising from financial relationships, that an investment
by a limited partner in the partnership also is an investment in
the controlling general partners. The Commission stated that the
same rationale could apply to two general partners in a regular
partnership or in a limited partnership.2

Your facts differ significantly from those in In re Nord.
You stated that Mr. Delaney only owns 2.5 percent of the LLC. In
addition, the LLC is managed by its executive officer, not
Mr. Delaney. Thus, it would not appear that Mr. Delaney is an
economic interest of yours. Absent an economic interest in
Mr. Delaney, you will only be prohibited from participating in
decisions foreseeably and materially affecting your other economic
interests, such as the LLC.

Foreseeability and Materiality

As noted above, for an economic interest to be disqualifying
with respect to a specific governmental decision, it must be
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, on the economic interest.

Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reason-
ably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

The test for materiality differs depending on the specific
circumstances of each decision. For example, where the LLC is
directly before the port commission, the effect is generally
deemed to be material and disqualification is required.3
(Regulation 18702.1(a).)

Where the LLC is not directly before the port commission, but
may be indirectly affected (such as here), Regulation 18702.2
applies. For example, if the LLC was a relatively small business
entity, Regulation 18702.2(g) provides that the effect of a
decision is material where:

_____________________
2 See In re Nord, at footnote 16.

3 An economic interest is directly before the port commission
when the economic interest initiates the proceeding by filing an
application, claim, appeal, or similar request, or is a named
party in, or the subject of, the proceeding. A person or business
entity is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject
person or business entity. (Regulation 18702.1(b).)

Our File No. A-95-137
Page 5




     (1)  The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $10,000 or more; or

     (2)  The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or

     (3)  The decision will result in the increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $10,000 or more.

     Thus, if subdivision (g) is the appropriate standard,4 you
may not participate in any decision that could foreseeably
increase or decrease the gross revenues, assets or liabilities of
the LLC by $10,000 or more, or increase or decrease expenses by
$2,500.

     If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5660.5

                                   Sincerely,

                                   Steven G. Churchwell
                                   General Counsel

                                   [signature]
                                   By:  John W. Wallace
                                        Counsel, Legal Division




_____________________________

4    If subdivision (g) is not applicable, the appropriate
subdivision in Regulation 18702.2 should be used to determine
whether the effect of a decision is material.

5    Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.  Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
